title: Trump administration announces new tariffs on more than 80 countries, claiming failed ‘forced labor’ enforcement – US politics live
summary: Canada, Mexico, Australia, EU, UK and China among dozens of economies listed in US president’s latest ⁠effort to restore regime of near-global import taxes after supreme court struck down his ‘Liberation Day’ tariffs
body: As soon as the Trump administration announced its new tariffs on 86 countries, for supposedly failing to crack down on forced labor, the office of the US trade representative, Jamieson Greer, turned its official, government social media account into a political battering ram: posting past statements against forced labor from 15 senators and members of Congress of both parties, in a transparent attempt to head off their criticism of the new tariffs. One of those lawmakers, congresswoman Linda Sánchez, a California Democrat, denounced the pretext for the new tariffs as a sham anyway. “President Trump isn’t serious about combating forced labor. He’s trying to make an end-run around the Supreme Court and Congress to reimpose his illegal tariffs,” Sánchez wrote on social media. She continued: If he was serious, he would not be applying the same tariff rate to China, one of the worst forced labor abusers in the world, as he does to countries like Australia. Then again, unlike China, Australia doesn’t manufacture any Trump corporation products. This is the same administration that gutted funding to combat forced labor through cuts to the Bureau of International Labor Affairs and rolled back worker protections here at home, while encouraging companies to profit off some of the world’s worst forced labor abusers through his proposed U.S.-China Board of Trade. We need to take forced labor seriously. But that includes addressing our problems here at home, including by taking on the export of goods made with American prison labor. Slapping more tariffs on every country that upsets the president isn’t the way to do it. The government of Brazil has rejected the Trump administration’s claim that it, and 85 other nations, had failed to enforce bans on forced labor, and called the new 12.5% US tariff on imports from Brazil “completely arbitrary and unjustified”. In a statement provided to Brazilian news outlets, including O Globo, president Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva’s government said that it would immediately trigger a process of imposing retaliatory tariffs on US goods. “Lacking a domestic legal basis to support its protectionist trade policy,” the Brazilian government said, the Trump administration “chose to manipulate an issue central to human rights and the struggle of workers worldwide in order to accuse 59 countries and the European Union of unfair practices”. Brazil also noted that it had cooperated with the investigation by the US trade representative’s office, and provided “extensive” documentation of Brazilian legislation and actions taken by customs authorities to curb imports of goods produced using forced labor. “The United States bans certain imports from China produced by forced labor. In effect, the Trump administration wants to globalize this ban,” Monica de Bolle, a Brazilian economist and senior fellow at the Peterson Institute for International Economics in Washington, wrote on Friday. “Brazil is one of the US targets, posing a problem since China is one of its biggest trading partners. But one thing is clear. This dispute is not about forced labor. None of the remedies being sought would do anything to address any forced labor conditions anywhere. No, this action is about the Trump team’s effort to use any legal stratagem to wage its trade war and to single out Brazil for punishment.” “Virtually every US ally – including the European Union, Canada, and Mexico – received materially identical USTR findings on the same day. Using slave labor as a bogus justification for a trade war becomes obvious when you realize that the tariff is being threatened against some 60 economies simultaneously,” she added. As nations are scrambling to find out if they are subject to the new tariffs imposed on Thursday by the Trump administration, for supposedly failing to crack down on forced labor, the US trade representative’s office has provided a list of nations or economic blocs supposedly guilty of a “Failure to Impose and Effectively Enforce a Prohibition on the Importation of Goods Produced with Forced Labor”. According to a draft version of the Federal Register Notice: On June 2, 2026, the Trade Representative determined that, in each of the 60 investigations, certain of the acts, polices, and practices of the economy at issue are actionable under sections 301(b) and 304(a) of the Trade Act. The Trade Representative also issued a comprehensive report, Acts, Policies, and Practices of Various Economies Related to the Failure to Impose and Effectively Enforce a Prohibition on the Importation of Goods Produced with Forced Labor (Report). Specifically, the Trade Representative determined that: • The following 54 economies have failed to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor: Algeria; Angola; Argentina; Australia; the Bahamas; Bahrain; Bangladesh; Brazil; Cambodia; Chile; China, People’s Republic of; Colombia; Costa Rica; Dominican Republic; Egypt; El Salvador; Guatemala; Guyana; Honduras; Hong Kong, China; India; Iraq; Israel; Japan; Jordan; Kazakhstan; Kuwait; Libya; Malaysia; Morocco; New Zealand; Nicaragua; Nigeria; Norway; Oman; Peru; the Philippines; Qatar; Russia; Saudi Arabia; Singapore; South Africa; South Korea; Sri Lanka; Switzerland; Taiwan; Thailand; Trinidad and Tobago; Türkiye; United Arab Emirates; United Kingdom; Uruguay; Venezuela; and Vietnam. • The following six economies have failed to effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor: Canada, Ecuador, the European Union, Indonesia, Mexico, and Pakistan. Since there are 27 countries in the European Union, a total of 86 nations are subject to the new US tariffs which go into effect on Friday. Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, just announced that the Trump administration “is imposing tariffs on more than 80 countries for their failure to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor.” Citing legal authority from Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, Greer said that the new tariffs come after investigations by his office. The new tariffs, at Donald Trump’s direction, are the president’s latest ⁠effort to restore a regime of near-global import taxes after after the US supreme court struck down his “Liberation Day” tariffs of 10% to 50% imposed last year ⁠under a national emergencies law the court said did not apply. Trump initially responded to that ruling by imposing a temporary 10% tariff for 150 days which expires ‌on Friday. In a statement, Greer called the new tariffs a response to human rights abuses, one day after the Trump administration struck a nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, which has been described by rights groups as one of the worst human rights offenders in the world. The kingdom, Human Rights Watch reported, has invested in “high-profile sports and entertainment events domestically and internationally” in order to “whitewash the country’s abysmal human rights record. “Migrant workers, including on projects and companies linked to the Saudi sovereign wealth fund, “face widespread abuses across employment sectors”. “President Trump recognizes that decades of moral suasion have not eradicated forced labor from global supply chains. The United States has had a forced labor import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it’s well past time for our trading partners to do the same,” Greer said in his statement on the new tariffs. “Today’s action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere.” In his prepared remarks at the Rose Garden event to honor the Los Angeles Dodgers for winning back-to-back World Series championships , Donald Trump ran through the highlights of the Dodgers’ championship-winning games and then veered off script to praise the real heroes: the team’s owners. Having done so, Trump then praised the late George Steinbrenner, who bought the New York Yankees in 1973. “If they manage to repeat this year, they would become the first team to win three in a row since the days of the legendary baseball dynasty, the 1990s New York Yankees. And I was there for a lot of those games with George Steinbrenner. He was a friend of mine, and he was the guy he liked winning. He would do anything to win.” Trump did not mention that the year after Steinbrenner bought the Yankees, he pled guilty to violating federal campaign finance law in a scheme to make illegal contributions to Richard Nixon’s re-election campaign, and instructing his employees to lie to the FBI and a grand jury in a failed attempt to hide his crime. In 1989, Ronald Reagan pardoned Steinbrenner for his crimes. Donald Trump has taken the stage and started speaking at an event to honor the Los Angeles Dodgers. Before mentioning the team, the president boasts about what he calls his improvements to the White House, including paving over the Rose Garden lawn and adding letters to the outside of the building, reading “The Rose Garden,” and creating what he calls the “Presidential Walk of Fame”, which features insults to his predecessors, Barack Obama and Joe Biden apparently penned by Trump himself. “You see the right up there,” Trump said pointing the words “The Rose Garden” in brass letters on the wall of the West Colonnade of the White House “I had them put that up, looks very nice,” Trump said. “Do you think Biden would do that? I don’t think so. I don’t think Biden would do it.” “And we created the Presidential Walk of Fame,” he went on. “That wall for 150 years had absolutely nothing on it. And it always used to amaze me. I said, ‘What are we going to put on that wall now?’” “We did from Washington to Trump and people love it,” Trump said. “And they read, they study the plaques. And generally speaking, I was very nice to the people up there. A couple of them probably not as nice as I could have been.” Th…

title: UK ‘ready to defend itself’ after Iranian threat over US bases
summary: IRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’
body: The government has said the UK is “ready to defend itself” after Iran’s military warned that any bases being used by the US were “legitimate targets”. The UK has allowed the US to launch “defensive” operations from British bases hosting American planes since the start of its war on Iran, but has refused to help in offensive operations. That policy has not been altered by the new prime minister, Andy Burnham, who was notified last week that a decision had been made to extend the agreement with the US. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said on Thursday that “any base used for aggression against Iranian territory constitutes a legitimate target for our forces”. In a statement published on the IRGC-linked Tasnim news agency, the group said American bombing missions had been launched from RAF Fairford in Gloucestershire two days earlier. The statement came after the US carried out a 12th consecutive night of strikes against targets in Iran. The Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether Fairford was used by the US for operations against Iran this week. The IRGC also accused the “British monarchy regime” of causing being the “primary cause of hardships in our region with a black record of partitioning Islamic nations, widespread massacres, imposing despotic regimes, and organising the occupation of Palestine”. It warned the government “not to weigh down its record any further”. A spokesperson for the British government said: “Our armed forces are ready to keep ⁠the United Kingdom safe from any kind of attacks, whether it’s on our soil ⁠or from abroad. The UK stands ready 24/7 to defend itself. “This includes through ‌operating a layered approach to air and missile defence, ‌provided by Royal Navy, British Army and Royal Air Force assets equipped with a range of advanced capabilities, working closely with our Nato allies. “We are committed to defending our people, our interests and our allies, acting in accordance with international law and not getting drawn into the wider conflict.” On 1 March a drone struck the RAF base at Akrotiri in Cyprus, hitting a hangar, and prompting a partial evacuation of the facility. On 19 March, Iran fired two ballistic missiles at the UK’s Diego Garcia base in the Chagos Islands. One reportedly failed mid-flight, while the other was shot down by a US airship. Iran has launched strikes against countries in the Gulf that house US military bases, including Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait, killing American service personnel in some of the attacks. Donald Trump said Iran would “pay a big price” for killing US troops. On Thursday afternoon, the US president said he was considering ordering a “massive attack” on Iran. “I am close to making a decision,” he told Axios. “We’re all set for it.” He said he believed Iran’s leaders “want to negotiate”, but aren’t yet ready to make a deal. In another sign of the conflict potentially escalating, Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels said they attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea on Thursday, as oil topped $100 a barrel.

title: Middle East crisis is ‘getting out of control’, UN chief warns, as fears mount of escalation spiral – live updates
summary: Secretary general António Guterres tells security council the Middle East is being pushed to the ‘edge of the unimaginable’ amid latest threats from Donald Trump
body: Donald Trump’s suggestion that the US could use Iranian frozen assets to pay for war damage comes as his administration threatened to step up attacks, and Yemen’s Houthis struck Saudi shipping and sent oil prices soaring. Trump said the US would hold Iran responsible for the Houthis’ actions and warned both would soon receive a “major military punishment”. On Thursday Brent crude surged past $100 a barrel for the first time since May, up 7%. European Central Bank president Christine Lagarde warned “the energy shock could intensify further” and push up inflation, feeding speculation about a September eurozone interest rate hike. German defence minister Boris Pistorius, meanwhile, warned of “dire consequences” for oil-importing economies and said “nothing – absolutely nothing – suggests that a ceasefire, let alone peace, could be reached”. Donald Trump has suggested that damage caused to shipping by Iranian attacks could be paid for using Tehran’s assets frozen overseas. Please let this statement serve to represent, until further notice, that from this point forth, any and all damages done to Ships, Cargo, or anything related thereto, will be paid for by Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls.” It’s unclear whether the US would be able to use frozen assets in such a way; any move would probably be challenged. Europe has faced an array of issues in its attempts to use Russian frozen assets to fund Ukraine. The UN secretary general, António Guterres, warned that the Middle East is being pushed to the “edge of the unimaginable” amid mounting fears over the trajectory of the conflict. “The situation is getting out of control. It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable. The region is being pulled into an ever-widening circle of confrontation. One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next,” Guterres told a security council meeting. “As this dynamic spreads, the political objectives are becoming increasingly obscured by the confrontation itself.” It comes after Donald Trump said he was close to deciding whether to launch “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran as the US president flounders in his attempts to get a grip on the spiralling crisis in the region. Speaking to Axios, the US president said: “I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it.” He acknowledged that such a decision would have consequences but said he had not yet made a final determination. Israel “would join in two minutes if I ask them to”, he added, before saying: “We don’t need anybody.” Trump’s comments followed the earlier threat of “major military punishment” against Iran and the Houthis, after the Iran-backed Yemeni militia’s attacks on two Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea. The Houthi attacks threatened the safety of a second major maritime route connecting the Suez canal to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. They also came amid reports that Iran had sent military advisers and drone parts to the Houthis 10 days ago. Trump wrote on social media: “If [the Houthis] do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis, themselves.” In a separate development, Trump clarified that an announced agreement that the US would help Saudi Arabia with its civilian nuclear programme would require the kingdom to normalise relations with Israel through the Abraham Accords, a move Saudi Arabia has long resisted until there is demonstrable movement on Palestinian rights and statehood. During Thursday’s press briefing, Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, reiterated that the Saudi Arabia nuclear deal is “contingent” on the kingdom on joining the Abraham Accords. “The president is always the final dealmaker, as you know... He has said, if they don’t join the Abraham accords, the deal is off,” she said. It emerged later that Trump had not spoken to Saudi Arabia about the new condition. Oil prices once again surged past the symbolic $100 for a barrel of Brent crude, the international benchmark, and amid indications the price could go higher still. “Whenever Trump starts taking about massive military action on Iran, everything changes in Iran overnight. Parents begin to stock up on food, groceries, and medicine; school administration fears opening the school, and children ask questions that no parent should have to answer. My biggest fear tonight is not anything but to watch another generation of Iranian children grow up believing that war is normal for us. We have already lived very difficultly through sanctions, inflation, and uncertainty. Another escalation and broader war would only deepen the anxiety that families carry with them every single day,” Sahar, a 38-year-old teacher from Tehran who uses only a single name, told the Guardian. “In hospitals and medical facilities, we prepare for emergencies whenever there is a political statement of potential attacks. Even if there are no attacks taking place, the psychological impact is immediate about such ultimatums. Normal patients and families become more anxious, healthcare workers and medics worry about shortages, and people delay treatment because they fear what may happen next. The human cost of these threats on Iranians begins long before any attacks actually start,” Neda, a 31-year-old doctor from Tabriz, told the Guardian. Iran’s top negotiator and parliamentary speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, has mocked the US military’s strategy aimed at degrading Tehran’s ability to control the strait of Hormuz, and suggested it has backfired. “They wanted to punish Iran. Punished themselves with triple-digit oil instead. 10/10 strategy,” Ghalibaf said on X. Oil prices rose above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months earlier on Thursday after the Houthis attacked two Saudi oil tankers, as a fresh escalation of the conflict threatens to compound disruption to global oil supplies. Sirens are sounding in Kuwait, the state news agency reports, as the Kuwaiti army says its air defences are currently intercepting hostile missile and drone attacks from Iran. The Gulf Cooperation Council, led by secretary general Jasem Mohamed Albudaiwi, has condemned the Houthi targeting of Encelia, a Saudi-owned tanker in the Red Sea. On Thursday, Albudaiwi said the attack “represents an extension of the hostile behavior pursued by the militia and a flagrant violation of international law.” He added that the Houthi’s “continued threats to target commercial vessels and jeopardise international navigation lanes constitute a danerous escalation” in the region. The attack marks a significant escalation in Middle East tensions and comes after Donald Trump vowed to destroy a bridge or power plant in Iran every time the country attacks a ship in the Hormuz Strait. Oman’s foreign minister Badr Albusaidi has met with his UK counterpart, Ed Miliband, on Thursday amid growing tensions across the Middle East as Donald Trump deliberates on further strikes against Iran. In a statement on X, Albusaidi said that the two “discussed the Strait of Hormuz and the urgent need for stakeholders to cooperate on UNCLOS obligations,” referring to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea. The meeting follows the UK’s decision to temporarily withdraw its diplomatic staff from Iran, citing “regional tensions" and “due to the security situation” in the Midle East which “remains unpredictable,” the UK government said on its website. The UN secretary general, António Guterres, has told the security council that the Middle East is being pushed to the “edge of the unimaginable” amid mounting fears over the trajectory of the conflict. “The situation is getting out of control. It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable. The region is being pulled into an ever-widening circle of confrontation. One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next,” Guterres told a security council meeting. “As this dynamic spreads, the political objectives are becoming increasingly obscured by the confrontation itself,” he said. It comes after Donald Trump said he was close to deciding whether to launch “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran as the US president flounders in his attempts to get a grip on the spiralling crisis in the region. Amid growing evidence that a major escalation may be imminent, the UK announced that it was temporarily withdrawing diplomatic staff from Iran on Wednesday, and the German military announced on Thursday that it would withdraw two vessels from the Red Sea. And as Israeli cities opened bomb shelters, the US state department warned Americans living in the region of the risk that flights may be cancelled. With Trump’s poll ratings in freefall, and his war against Iran deeply unpopular at home, the US president and his administration are running short of time before the midterm elections in the autumn to force a resolution to the conflict on Washington’s terms. Donald Trump posted on Truth Social that he will hold Iran responsible for attacked launched by the Houthi militia. This came after the Yemen-based, Iran-backed militia said it attacked two Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea, with a Saudi news agency later confirming one of the vessels was ablaze on Thursday. The Houthis have declared a maritime blockade of Saudia Arabia, a move that current and former US officials said could significantly widen the US-Iran war and strain the American military. Reuters reported that Iran flew Islamic Revolutionary ⁠Guard Corps (IRGC) commanders, military advisers and missile- and drone-related equipment into Yemen this month, which suggests that Tehran is seeking to strengthen its Houthi allies…

title: Trump seizes on New Jersey election error to boost misleading fraud claims
summary: Republicans have latched on to the announcement after Trump’s lie-laden speech, but experts say DHS’s data is ‘designed to create a false argument’
body: Donald Trump and his allies quickly seized on an announcement from Mikie Sherrill, the New Jersey governor, on Tuesday that 6,600 noncitizens had registered to vote because of a software error, and fewer than 400 of them voted, using the disclosure to escalate false claims that there are scores of noncitizens on the voter rolls. Trump seized on the news days after he said in a primetime speech that the Department of Homeland Security identified 278,000 noncitizens on the voter rolls in New Jersey and three other states. But a Guardian review of the letters the DHS sent the states shows the agency expressed less certainty about the number and has offered no explanation of its methodology. Since 2018, New Jersey has had automatic voter registration, which means that any time someone interacts with a motor vehicle office they are automatically registered to vote, unless they opt out. Between 2023 and 2024, some 6,600 people had indicated they were not citizens, yet nonetheless had been registered to vote. About 400 of those people cast a ballot, Sherrill said. Harmeet Dhillon, the head of the justice department’s civil rights division, sent a letter to Sherrill earlier this week requesting the names of those who were registered and voted and the department is reportedly mulling prosecutions. Sherrill suggested on Thursday the state wasn’t going to turn over the information on voters and said earlier in the week it would be unfair to prosecute them for an error that was the state’s fault. The number represents a small fraction of the more than 6.6 million registered voters in the state. But within hours of the announcement, Trump and other election deniers inflated the number of people who registered to vote because of the error, and held it up as yet another data point that “illegals” are stealing American elections on behalf of Democrats. Trump said in a post on Truth Social: “35,152 is the new number just released by Homeland Security of non-citizens registered to vote in New Jersey. These are just the ones that got caught. The real numbers will prove to be many times this amount. They are all Dumocrats!.” There were Democrats and Republicans erroneously registered, Sherrill said on Tuesday, with the majority unaffiliated with a party. The incident is far from proof of the widespread fraud that Republicans allege, experts say. “It’s not good, I don’t want to suggest otherwise. I don’t want to tell anyone that they’re stupid for thinking it’s less than perfectly optimal. But it’s not really an example of what they’re talking about,” said Stephen Richer, a Republican who oversaw elections in Maricopa county, Arizona, from 2021 to 2025. “These people were trying to do the right thing and acknowledge that they’re not citizens, and then the vast majority of them, even though registered and presumably [were] sent election mail materials, ignored that and didn’t vote.” The number of non-citizens the state says were registered represents a “minuscule fraction” of New Jersey’s active registered voters in 2024, said David Becker, an election administration expert and the executive director of the Center for Election Innovation and Research. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes. “While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” he said. The firestorm over the New Jersey voter rolls came as Trump escalated his incendiary rhetoric about voting ahead of the midterm elections. Last week, in a primetime address from the White House, he offered what seemed to be an eye-popping statistic: the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) had found 278,000 non-citizens on the rolls in just four states – California, Nevada, Pennsylvania and New Jersey. That, too, is a misleading figure. In its letters to election officials in those four states DHS expressed less certainty about the 278,000 number, saying it was a ceiling of how many noncitizens could be on the rolls. The agency also offered little to no explanation of its methodology for identifying noncitizens on the rolls. It gave the states two weeks to respond. “It’s a laughable letter,” Becker said. “Imagine someone said to you: ‘Some of the items in your fridge are poisonous. Write us back in two weeks and tell us what you’re going to do about that.’” The first question one would ask, Becker said, is “which items?” “They’re not serious. They don’t want the states to even maintain their records correctly. If they did, they would be completely transparent, and they would have acted immediately and said we absolutely need you to do this,” he added. “This is designed to create a false argument post-election that the election was stolen. This has nothing to do with actually cleaning up the lists.” The noncitizen registration claim was part of a slew of misleading information Trump offered in a speech last week designed to weaken confidence in the US election system ahead of November midterm elections in which his party is expected to lose seats. It also comes as Republicans are pushing legislation that would impose new proof-of-citizenship requirements on voter registration, even though several studies have shown noncitizen voting is extremely rare. Last week, both Trump and Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, cited the 278,000 number as evidence that states should share sensitive voter information with the federal government, with Mullin threatening potential prison time for those who don’t comply. At least one Republican has cited the number as a bona fide example of voter fraud. “We know there are roughly 278,000 noncitizens registered to vote. The last three presidential elections were decided by fewer votes,” Marsha Blackburn, a senator of Tennessee, said on Tuesday. “We can affirm that on its face, we refute these claims. These numbers are wildly speculative at best and the Department of Homeland Security hasn’t shared anything that backs it up,” said Cisco Aguilar, a Democrat serving as Nevada’s secretary of state. As of Wednesday afternoon, the DHS had not provided Nevada with a list of suspected noncitizens in the state. In a letter to New Jersey officials last week, the DHS said there may be as many as 35,152 noncitizens on the state’s voter rolls. The agency said it had matched 19,497 voter records to a noncitizen in its system, but did not offer an explanation for the rest. Sherrill said she had been informed of the voter registration issue in her state and begun investigating before Trump’s speech on Thursday evening. She said the DHS had provided no information to back up its claims. “We have asked them for any evidence of this, where they got that number, who this is. We’ve received nothing back yet,” she said on Tuesday. Understanding the methodology the DHS used is critical to assessing the veracity of its claims, experts say. Matching records across different databases is difficult, and the system the DHS uses to try and identify noncitizens – Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (Save) – is known to be error-prone. The DHS said it relied on publicly available voter information from the four states to try and identify noncitizens, information that probably lacks the level of detail needed to get a solid match between voter records and DHS data, Charles Stewart III, a professor at MIT and an expert in election administration, wrote in a post on Substack. “DHS likely lacked the identifiers needed for high-quality matching. Save checks generally require name, date of birth, and a unique immigration or Social Security identifier, and public voter files ordinarily do not contain the most sensitive identifiers,” he wrote. “Anyone with experience in these data-matching exercises should expect those numbers to melt as quickly as ice cream in a Phoenix August.” Even though the agency claimed there could be up to 278,000 noncitizens on their rolls, the DHS said in its letters to the states that it was able to match the name, date of birth, address and social security number to a noncitizen in its system for about 118,000 people. The agency offered no explanation for why it believed there were an additional 160,000 noncitizens on the rolls The DHS did not respond to multiple requests for comment on its methodology. “I am about as certain as I can be, without them coming up with some magical data technique that nobody has ever seen before, that these numbers cannot be even remotely credible,” Becker said. “What I can’t tell you is whether they just made them up. Because they have not been transparent about that. And it’s possible that they just made them up, because we just haven’t seen any methodology whatsoever.” Claims of widespread noncitizen registration and voting often start with a headline-grabbing statistic, and then the numbers often whittle down once election officials investigate. In 2019, for example, Texas announced it suspected there were nearly 100,000 noncitizens on its rolls, 58,000 of whom may have voted. The state later admitted the claim was based on bad data and agreed not to cancel anyone’s voter registration based on it.

title: D4vd lawyers challenge police testing of evidence found in singer’s home
summary: Defense attorneys question LAPD criminalist on third day of hearing to decide whether singer will stand trial
body: Attorneys for the singer D4vd scrutinized how law enforcement officials chose to test evidence found in his home during the third day of a hearing to determine whether he will stand trial for murder and child sexual abuse. Prosecutors say D4vd, born David Anthony Burke, fatally stabbed 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez last year to prevent her from exposing the sexual nature of their relationship amid his rising profile in the music world. Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges of first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under 14, and mutilation of human remains. Earlier in the week, prosecutors presented evidence that possible traces of blood were detected in areas of Burke’s former Hollywood Hills residence and his Tesla. On Thursday, defense attorney Blair Berk questioned Lauren Wallace, a criminalist for the Los Angeles police department, about why certain items, including chainsaws located in the musician’s garage, were not tested. Berk appeared to suggest that another individual’s DNA could have been detected. “You were aware that multiple people lived at this residence, right?” she asked Wallace, in reference to the Hollywood Hills address, where the singer resided. “Yes, we were aware,” Wallace said. The exchange previewed how Burke’s lawyers might aim to poke holes in the prosecution’s case should it proceed to trial. On Wednesday, Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, testified about a Caltrans worker finding a passport bearing Rivas Hernandez’s name and photograph in January 2026 on a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara. Buenrostro arrived on scene after the worker called law enforcement about the identification card. During cross-examination, defense attorney Regina Peter appeared to cast doubt on possible theories of how the passport landed there: “Did you do anything to rule out the possibility that the card arrived there by means of some sort of storm or weather issue?” she asked. Prosecutors brought a detective to the stand on Thursday who testified that cellular data and a Tesla system showed Burke traveled to the area where Rivas Hernandez’s passport was discovered. The hearing is expected to conclude by early next week. The Associated Press contributed reporting

title: Regional mayors to take key roles in UK policy decisions as part of No 10 North plans
summary: Burnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic council
body: Regional mayors are to be given a leading role in the UK’s economic policy, Andy Burnham has said, as he called the opening of No 10 North “the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen”. The new prime minister has revived the national economic council [NEC], first set up by Gordon Brown to put the government on a war footing during the 2008 financial crash, arguing that economic growth requires the same level of urgency, focus and collective action. In an article for the Guardian, he wrote: “When I entered Downing Street, I told staff that the era of fighting devolution is over. No one in government loses when power is shared.” Burnham will chair the inaugural meeting of the council on Friday, bringing together senior ministers and regional mayors to put devolution at the heart of his government’s mission. Burnham wrote: “Even though Whitehall departments have set up outposts, decision-making power has remained in London … This is what No 10 North is seeking to correct and, if we succeed, it could bring the much-needed sea change in the way Britain is run. “It is about driving growth from the most powerful office in the land and doing so in close alignment with the Treasury. It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.” As he becomes the first British leader to run the government on a weekly basis from Manchester, Burnham will face calls to prove that the operation is not tokenistic. Earlier this week, he created a powerful new prime minister’s department taking in parts of the Cabinet Office, Treasury and housing ministry to become the “dominant driver of the UK’s economy” – an apparent tension with the No 10 North plan. In one of his first acts, Burnham set up the Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet, to be led by his ally and enforcer Louise Haigh, which will oversee No 10 North in Manchester, the No 10 operation in London, and parts of the Cabinet Office. Haigh, along with the education secretary, Lucy Powell, and the communities secretary, Angela Rayner – who both have Greater Manchester seats – are planning to spend some of their time working from No 10 North, while teams of civil servants are expected to be relocated there. With regional mayors invited to sit round the table, Downing Street argues that devolution will be cemented at the centre of economic decision-making, maintaining the prime minister’s pledge that decisions will be taken with local leaders, not just for them. Operating from Heron House in Manchester’s city centre, the home of GCHQ in the north-west, No 10 North is intended to bring together the levers of local economic growth and devolution under one roof for the first time, working from a single plan rather than competing ones. Burnham is expected to work from No 10 North each week so that decisions about the country’s economic future will no longer exclusively be made in Westminster, with other ministers also working regularly from the building. Dave Penman, the general secretary of the FDA union which represents senior civil servants, has welcomed the changes to the structure of government but cautioned that relocation was a big decision for staff. At least seven Greater Manchester Combined Authority employees, including Burnham’s deputy chief of staff, Caroline Simpson, and longstanding aide Kevin Lee, are already moving into the temporary building, while a brownfield site is developed. The Heron House office will be led by Sam Lister, a senior civil servant who has worked across several departments. The NEC’s attenders will change at each meeting, depending on the issues under consideration, bringing together business leaders, trade unions and other partners where their expertise can help drive decision-making. Under Gordon Brown, the body met twice a week and acted as the focus of his government’s effort to stave off recession, the global crisis in the financial markets and volatile prices, although there were concerns at the time that it could clip the wings of the Treasury. The new committee was quickly described as an economic version of the Cobra emergency committee. It included key figures such as the then chancellor Alistair Darling and former business secretary Peter Mandelson, and was instrumental in coordinating emergency banking sector bailouts. In his article, Burnham suggested that the Manchester department would be crucial to his aim to “drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK”. He went on: “It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council house building to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way. “Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.”

title: Trump administration to impose fresh wave of tariffs on more than 80 countries
summary: US will impose between 10 and 12.5% tariff on countries that are top trading partners, US trade representative says
body: Donald Trump’s administration has imposed a fresh wave of sweeping tariffs to replace a 10% global duty that was set to expire early Friday morning, the latest stage of his aggressive trade policy. The US will impose between a 10% or 12.5% tariff on more than 80 countries, Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative announced late on Thursday. The duties would fall under section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, which is aimed against countries that engage in forced labor. “Today’s action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere,” Greer said in a statement. “I am encouraged by the trading partners who have moved quickly to adopt forced labor import prohibitions, and look forward to ensuring their effective enforcement.” Trump’s tariffs policy suffered a damaging blow in February when the US supreme court ruled that many of those tariffs were illegal. While the US replaced those tariffs with a 10% tariff regime on much of the world, these are due to expire on Friday. Trump and his officials have claimed his controversial trade agenda will help revitalize US industrial heartlands and boost jobs. But his tariff policies have faced several setbacks since they were first instituted last year, and critics have warned his sweeping tariffs risk increasing prices nationwide. US inflation surged to a three-year high earlier this year. During a heated exchange before US senators on Wednesday, however, Greer appeared to claim Trump’s policies had not driven prices higher. Asked by the Democratic senator Elizabeth Warren if the tariffs had increased prices for American families, Greer said: “No.” “Core inflation fell to 2.6% year on year, much better than in January 2025,” he added. Core inflation excludes food and energy. Overall inflation is slightly higher than it was when Joe Biden left office. More details soon …

title: Louisiana House candidate accused of rape says he hopes to again ‘earn’ Trump endorsement
summary: Republican Blake Miguez is weighing what district to run in after state redrew map following supreme court decision
body: A congressional candidate from Louisiana who was the subject of a police report accusing him of rape – which he denies – says he is hoping to again “earn” Donald Trump’s endorsement as he considers precisely where to run amid redistricting. The candidate, Blake Miguez, is weighing running in one of two districts, neither of which he lives in but in which he is allowed to run. He has been mulling that choice after the supreme court’s landmark decision in Callais v Louisiana prompted the state to swiftly redraw its US House districts – and after he had secured the president’s backing under the previous congressional map. Approached on Wednesday after Miguez’s campaign website had become password protected and otherwise blank, a spokesperson said: “Blake was honored to have President Trump’s endorsement for [an] old … congressional district that no longer exists” after Callais, “and [he] hopes to earn it again after he announces a campaign under the new district lines.” A White House spokesperson clarified that Trump did not formally rescind his endorsement – but added that the president “will look at the candidate field after candidate [qualifying] if necessary”. “At this point, [Trump] has not made any changes nor does he have an intent to,” the spokesperson said. “If new information changes things, we will cross that bridge when we get to it.” The Republican Louisiana state senator secured the coveted backing of Trump in the race for a US House seat as he qualified to run in a 16 May primary for the post, which is safely held by their party. But that primary was postponed until the November midterms after the Callais decision. Miguez’s campaign has also had to grapple with an Atlantic report that he had not disclosed to Trump’s team the existence of a 2007 rape accusation reported to local law enforcement on the same day of the alleged assault. That police report – which the Guardian also independently obtained and reported on – never resulted in charges, and Miguez categorically denied the claim as false. But it reportedly raised concerns within the White House that Miguez “either wasn’t fully vetted or wasn’t forthcoming about discoverable documents from his past” while he successfully pursued Trump’s endorsement, as the Atlantic reported. The statements from Miguez’s campaign and the White House sparked verbal sparring from one of the candidate’s Republican rivals. Lionel Rainey, a spokesperson for a political action committee supporting another candidate in the postponed US House race, Rick Edmonds, said he found it “silly” to blame redistricting for evident uncertainty over Trump’s endorsement. “Nobody believes that,” Rainey said. Separate 12 March articles from the Guardian and the Atlantic reported on a woman who alleged being raped by Miguez in late August 2007 when he purportedly was her “live-in ex boyfriend”. A police report quoted the woman as saying the assault took place at the end of a night of drinking in Miguez’s home town of Erath, Louisiana. But the legal document said she did not want to press charges against Miguez – then in law school – because she did not want to get him into trouble. One of the ways the Miguez campaign responded to those claims was to link to an article from a Louisiana news outlet that followed up on the Atlantic and the Guardian’s reporting with quotes from family members of the accuser. Those family members questioned the accuser’s credibility while citing mental health and substance abuse problems. The campaign, meanwhile, also circulated an email attributed to her father that was sent to the state senator’s office which read in part: “Everything my daughter has reported about you were lies and she is a liar and has a drug problem.” The Guardian subsequently wrote to the woman offering her the opportunity to address her family member’s statements. She did not reply. In the months since the Miguez accuser’s claims, US House representatives Eric Swalwell and Tony Gonzales – a California Democrat and a Texas Republican, respectively – resigned under bipartisan pressure over allegations of sexual misconduct against both. Furthermore, on 10 July, the Democratic US Senate candidate Graham Platner of Maine withdrew his scandal-laden candidacy days after detailed sexual assault allegations were made against him. Miguez won election to Louisiana’s state senate in 2023 after more than eight years in its house of representatives. He signed up to contest for a seat that congresswoman Julia Letlow vacated when, with Trump’s backing, she victoriously ran in a Republican US Senate primary at the expense of incumbent Bill Cassidy. The boundaries of that seat – in Louisiana’s fifth congressional district – were outside Miguez’s Erath home when Trump endorsed him in February as a “warrior” of the president’s Maga movement. Miguez’s opponents seized on that to try to cast him as a political opportunist, and his campaign countered that he has also long kept a home as well as operated a business in the fifth district, which then included Baton Rouge, Louisiana’s capital, as the state’s Shreveport Times reported. Under the state’s post-Callais congressional map, Erath is still outside Louisiana’s fifth congressional district. The town is in Louisiana’s third district, which since 2017 has been represented by the Republican Clay Higgins. But the new sixth congressional district contains the Baton Rouge capital region. And on Thursday, the sixth district incumbent – the Democrat Cleo Fields – said that he would not seek re-election under the altered boundaries of the post-Callais map. The Times reported in June that Miguez opponents had sources telling them that he had asked Trump to let him carry the president’s fifth district endorsement over to the sixth. US House candidates are not required to run in their home districts. They only need to live in the same state as the congressional district in which they are running. Miguez’s campaign on Wednesday declined to say which Louisiana congressional district he may target when a three-day qualifying period opens on 5 August. • Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organizations. In the US, Rainn offers support on 800-656-4673. In the UK, Rape Crisis offers support on 0808 500 2222. In Australia, support is available at 1800Respect (1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found at ibiblio.org/rcip/internl.html

title: Trump says nearly 200 firms have signed pledge to protect Americans from costs arising from datacenters
summary: ‘Ratepayer Protection Pledge’ president has touted is non-binding as people continue to struggle with rising bills
body: Donald Trump has announced that about 200 entities have signed on to his non-binding “Ratepayer Protection Pledge”, expanding a voluntary commitment which claims to ensure US consumers will not bear the cost of the AI datacenter build-out. Trump delivered remarks on Thursday at the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) headquarters, alongside Lee Zeldin, the agency’s administrator, and Chris Wright, the energy secretary. Other attenders included governors who signed on to the pledge, including Brian Kemp of Georgia, Mike DeWine of Ohio, Spencer Cox of Utah and Jeff Landry of Louisiana. The pledge, first announced in March, was initially signed by seven tech companies: Google, Microsoft, Meta, Oracle, xAI, OpenAI and Amazon. It will now cover companies delivering 80% of all power to US homes and businesses, according to the White House. “We have kept rates down, way down,” said Trump. Twenty-three Republican governors have signed on to the scheme, Trump announced on Thursday, as have major utilities including Nextera and Duke Energy, datacenter developers and electricity cooperatives. “Electricity rates for 80% of the power distributed in America are now being kept in check by the plan,” Trump said at a press conference. The announcement came amid a growing backlash to the artificial intelligence boom and the datacenters powering it, as electricity bills rise ahead of November’s midterm elections. In June, utility prices were up 4% year-over-year, data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics shows. On Thursday, Trump said “radical left communists” are the ones who “want to shut down all new development, kill our most successful industries, destroy hundreds of thousands of jobs, and surrender the AI race to China”. Yet opposition to datacenters has become a bipartisan issue, with voters concerned about not only utility rates, but also the environmental and social impacts of AI. More than a dozen states have considered moratoria on datacenters, and New York became the first US state to enact a temporary ban this month. Senator Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, a representative, have also proposed a national moratorium. And on Thursday, Greg Abbott, the Republican governor of Texas, called for a ban on datacenter development in rural Texas; he is now among the governors who signed on to the pledge. The pledge will “ensure everyone involved in building and powering data centers covers their own costs instead of passing them on to American families”, Taylor Rogers, a White House spokesperson, told the Guardian. “The President’s bold action is turning datacenters into engines of growth for local communities, while cementing America’s dominance in the global AI race,” Rogers said. Green groups are largely unimpressed with the non-binding pledge. “The American people don’t care about the weak pledges and empty promises; they need relief from soaring energy bills month after month and real action from these companies to pay their fair share and bring down high costs,” said Patrick Drupp, climate policy director at the national environmental advocacy organization Sierra Club. “Today’s pledge is a paper-thin commitment to families who are struggling to keep the lights on as bills soar. The companies signing on have done nothing to show their work on how they will lower costs.” Lena Moffitt, executive director of climate advocacy organization Evergreen Action, called Trump’s announcement a “photo op with big tech” that “won’t lower a single family’s electric bill”. “Trump promised again and again to cut Americans’ energy costs, but instead he’s letting the same corporations driving up demand write their own rules – a voluntary pledge with no teeth and no consequences if they break it,” she said.

title: Southern California suburb alarmed as ‘exceptionally rare’ cancer sickens multiple children
summary: Residents in Ladera Ranch in Orange county voice concerns after six children diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma
body: Concerns are growing in southern California over a cluster of “exceptionally rare” cancer cases that has sickened multiple children in a suburban community. Families living in the Orange county neighborhood of Ladera Ranch, home to about 26,000 people, have reported that six children in the community were diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma, Katrina Foley, the vice-chair of the county board of supervisors, said in a statement. Ewing sarcoma is a rare cancer that primarily affects bone and soft tissue and is largely seen in young adults and children. At a community meeting this week, hundreds of residents turned up to voice their concerns, including several who spoke about additional cancer cases in adults and pets. While the American Cancer Society reports there are “no known lifestyle-related or environmental causes” of Ewing sarcoma, many residents believe the cancer cases are linked to the use of herbicides and pesticides. “We’re very concerned about pesticides that are being sprayed all around the community,” Liesl Heizt told ABC7. “There’s so many times that I walk around and they’re spraying, and I ask what they’re spraying and they’re not telling us.” During the meeting, Brian Dalton spoke about his daughter Lily who died earlier this year after being diagnosed with the same rare cancer as the other cases, KTLA 5 reported. “I don’t want another mother holding her child’s hand through endless treatments, praying for one more birthday, planning a funeral instead of a future,” Dalton said. In a letter sent last week, Foley urged the state to expedite a review of the cases and give residents “timely, transparent updates”. “Ladera Ranch families deserve answers, transparency and urgency from the state,” Foley said. “We do not yet know what caused these cancers, but that uncertainty demands a comprehensive review of every credible factor, including possible environmental exposures, along with direct communication and the public release of the findings.” Last week, Bill Essayli, an assistant US attorney, asked the Environmental Protection Agency to investigate the cluster to determine if there is an environmental cause behind the cases. At least six children have been diagnosed with the rare bone and soft-tissue cancer, he wrote. “The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and National Cancer Institute recognize that unusual patterns of cancer and community concerns regarding potential environmental exposures should be evaluated through a systematic, science-based process,” Essayli said.

title: Kemi Badenoch ‘has to adapt’ her rhetoric to take on Andy Burnham
summary: Conservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponent
body: “So, Andy Burnham seems like a nice guy.” Those were the slightly unexpected opening words of Kemi Badenoch’s first speech since the change in prime minister. But make no mistake: she was there to bury her opponent. Even Badenoch-sceptics inside the Conservative party and farther afield agree the Tory leader is becoming more confident, and has been able to forge an increasingly coherent message for her party, one based on a promise of economic competence. She does, nonetheless, face a different challenge now Burnham has replaced Keir Starmer. Aside from being a fresh face with a likely polling bounce, the former Greater Manchester mayor is more of a media natural, with the ability to deflect Badenoch’s jibes using self-deprecating humour, such as a viral social media response last month after the Tory leader dismissed him as a “pair of eyelashes and a black T-shirt”. “She has to adapt – it’s like with anything when your opponent changes,” one senior Conservative said. “As a personality, Burnham is much more likable [than Starmer], and comes with a fair degree of popularity. She needs to wait for weaknesses, and one immediately seems to be: where is the money coming from for all these giveaways?” And that was one of the main themes of Badenoch’s speech on Thursday morning, held in muggy sunshine on a rooftop terrace in central London. Labelling Burnham’s philosophy as “cap one price and send the bill somewhere else”, she repeatedly questioned how the new prime minister would pay for his early flurry of policies. The other central theme was ambition, Badenoch calling Burnham a prime minister “with small answers to big problems”. Such message discipline would be necessary in taking on the new operation in No 10, one senior Conservative MP said, especially given Badenoch’s occasional tendency to get caught up in culture-war debates. The MP said: “When she first became leader there was a bit of a worry that she would just talk endlessly about culture-war issues. But that hasn’t happened. The focus should be on the economy and jobs, which it currently is.” A manifestation of Badenoch’s increased confidence is a schedule of regular press conferences and lengthy media Q&As, often without a particular policy to announce. It is notably similar to the timetable maintained last summer by Nigel Farage – before his byelection battle and anxieties over questions about his finances scuppered this. And while she still maintains a largely take-no-prisoners approach, Badenoch now has rhetorical registers other than the sometimes caustic antagonism she used on Starmer and, in a speech which some Tories say began her turnaround, towards Rachel Reeves when she eviscerated the then-chancellor’s budget with almost startling ferocity. Such an approach would not necessarily work against politicians more relaxed in their own skin, said Luke Tryl, the UK director of the pollsters More in Common, who is a regular observer of its focus groups. “If Burnham is seen to just laugh it off it might be less effective than against brittleness,” Tryl said. “The reason it worked so well against Reeves, and her budget response got such rave reviews, was in part because Reeves looked so shellshocked. I suspect [the new chancellor] John Healey and Burnham won’t.” Bigger questions remain for Badenoch, not least why her own improved personal poll ratings have yet to translate into more support for her party, and whether her mission to reshape the Tories in her muscularly rightwing approach could put off some more moderate voters. But for now, much of the coming weeks will be devoted to pinning down how best to tackle a PM who is a notably more slippery target that his predecessor. “She has a summer to figure this out,” the senior Tory said. “But she has to be nimble – what works in July might not by September.”

title: Lobbying firm pressed for details about payments to journalists for favourable stories
summary: MPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clients
body: A political lobbying firm secretly paying journalists for articles favourable to its clients is facing calls to reveal full details of the payments, after the practice was denounced as a dangerous “attack on democracy”. MPs, senior lobbyists and transparency campaigners all condemned the behaviour of CT Group, the firm co-founded by the campaign strategist Lynton Crosby, an election guru previously hired by David Cameron and Boris Johnson. There were calls for an overhaul of lobbying rules and demands for CT Group to reveal the full extent of the practice, exposed after a four-month Guardian investigation. “Lobbyists paying journalists to produce apparently independent articles is an attack on the ethics of journalism, public relations and, indeed, on democracy,” said Alastair McCapra, the chief executive of the Chartered Institute of Public Relations. CT Group is not a member of the body. “Anyone can practise public relations in this country without belonging to a professional body or answering to a code of conduct. The majority do that ethically but these reported practices undermine our profession and erode the credibility on which responsible lobbying and public relations depend.” Lobbyists for CT Group told an undercover reporter they had paid journalists to place apparently independent stories in newspapers. They also offered hundreds of pounds in cash incentives to do the same for a property industry client and said that in return for the payment, CT Group would have increased editorial control. Jemimah Steinfeld, the chief executive of Index on Censorship, said: “To spin what is lobbying as journalism is shortsighted and frankly dangerous.” She called on CT Group to launch an internal investigation. Steve Goodrich, the head of research and investigations at Transparency International UK, said: “When lobbyists secretly pay journalists to write favourable coverage for their clients, it undermines the integrity of the press. This investigation raises serious questions about the conduct of CT Group, and about how widespread these hidden arrangements are across the lobbying industry.” Luke Taylor, a Liberal Democrat member of parliament’s public administration and constitutional affairs committee, said there should now be “full transparency on when money has changed hands when it comes to reporting”. “Across the board we are seeing worrying signs of our democracy being in peril,” he said. “A truly free press is a fundamental pillar of a healthy democracy and so we must do everything in our power to maintain it.” Sam Carling, a Labour MP on the committee, said: “If secret payments or other ethical failings are taking place widely, then the government ought to consider giving the Office of the Registrar of Consultant Lobbyists regulatory powers to hold lobbyists to account.” Rupa Huq, a Labour member of the Commons media select committee, said: “We need more transparency in lobbying which almost feels like the wild west, such is the lack of regulation.” She added: “I hope the new Andy Burnham government can grip this issue.” The Guardian began investigating the arrangements after CT Group approached a freelance journalist and offered to pay him to pitch a piece to a British national newspaper about problems caused for the housebuilding industry by the Building Safety Regulator, set up in the wake of the Grenfell Tower fire that killed 72 people. CT Group is a lobbying company with a £40m-a-year turnover whose recent clients include the tobacco firm Philip Morris and the owner of the football club Manchester City. In a statement, CT Group said: “We do not comment publicly on work we do for clients and we therefore cannot respond to the specific allegations you have made.” It said the events had been “deliberately mischaracterised” and that it had not engaged the undercover journalist to do anything and did not pay him anything. It said the journalist was looking for work and “chased us relentlessly over a period of more than six months”. The investigation has also raised concerns about the vulnerability of freelance journalism, which can often feature irregular and low-paying work. “If reporters are being paid to place stories in papers by private companies this breaches the essential buffer zone between lobbying and journalism,” said Martin Bright, a senior lecturer in journalism at the University of Essex. “The market is very tough for freelance journalists in the present media climate. They should never be exploited in this way.”

title: Secret Service agent on Vance’s detail under investigation
summary: Agent came under suspicion after allegedly providing info in MS Now report about vice-president’s helicopter trip
body: A Secret Service agent assigned to JD Vance’s protective detail is “the subject of an administrative investigation, and potential criminal inquiry, involving allegations of compromising operational and information security”, according to the Secret Service chief of communications. The agent came under suspicion after allegedly providing information that appeared in an MS Now report about the vice-president’s planned Marine Corps helicopter trip with his son to a golf lesson, a source told CNN. The report also claimed there was a “building morale problem” among the agents assigned to protect Vance and his family. The trip was ultimately called off due to severe thunderstorms. The source said the publication of those travel plans triggered unease among the Secret Service, the FBI and White House officials, who “fumed over operational details being described in the press”. A second source told CNN that investigators identified the individual they believe was responsible for leaking the information. The Secret Service confirmed the investigation in a statement shared with the Guardian, but did not specify what information the agent is accused of disclosing. “A member of the Vice Presidential Protective Division is the subject of an administrative investigation, and potential criminal inquiry, involving allegations of compromising operational and information security,” Anthony Guglielmi, the Secret Service chief of communications, said in the statement. “While we will not comment on the specifics of this matter, one principle is unequivocal: any conduct that potentially threatens the safety of a protectee will not be tolerated,” he added. The agent’s case is now being handled by the agency’s internal affairs component, presumably the Department of Justice’s office of professional responsibility. It is not yet known whether the investigation will lead to internal disciplinary action, criminal charges or both. The investigation comes as the Trump administration continues an aggressive push against what it says are unauthorized leaks to the media. Earlier this month, the New York Times reported that the Trump administration subpoenaed its reporters as part of a leak investigation involving a story about issues with the aircraft that Trump received as a gift from Qatar.

title: Take Centrica’s gas storage warning seriously – Britain still needs secure supplies
summary: One task awaiting new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh is devising a plan to avoid a crisis by the end of the decade
body: Welcome to the energy secretary job, Miatta Fahnbulleh. You have a politically tricky decision to make on whether to approve production at the Jackdaw gasfield and the Rosebank oilfield. But here’s another one that can’t wait much longer: what is the broader plan to ensure Britain avoids a gas supply crisis in 2030? By way of reminder, one of the more alarming reports published by the energy department under Ed Miliband was the one that warned of “an emerging risk” to gas supplies at the turn of the decade if an important piece of kit – an import terminal, say, or the critical 725-mile Langeled pipeline from Norway – were to be out of the action during a severe cold snap. “In the unlikely event of the loss of the single largest piece of gas infrastructure, gas supply falls short of demand for all pathways in 2030-31,” said the report from the government’s in-house adviser, the National Energy System Operator (Neso). Unlikely maybe – but the risk can’t be wished away, which was the point of the report. Now here comes Centrica, owner of British Gas, warning that it will close its Rough gas storage facility off the coast of Yorkshire unless it can agree a long-term financial support deal with the government to back a £2bn expansion. Rough is Britain’s largest such facility and, although Centrica has been issuing dire warnings for a while, it clearly means it this time. It has set a date for closure next April, in the absence of a deal. The company isn’t even injecting gas into Rough for this winter. “I think this [Rough] is essential for the UK energy security,” said Centrica’s chief executive, Chris O’Shea, on Thursday. “I think it would not be good if this was to be allowed to close, and that’s why it’s really a decision for government now. What we hear a lot is that this is a commercial decision for Centrica. That is patently not true.” On his commercial point, O’Shea is clearly correct. The economics of storage rely on buying low and selling high, but winter/summer spreads are often narrower these days. Disruption caused by the closure of the strait of Hormuz worked in Centrica’s favour at Rough as it made a £57m top-line profit at the facility in the half-year, but last year it lost almost as much. That is not a basis on which to sanction a £2bn investment without some form of regulated return. Whether Rough is the right storage project for the country is another matter. Gas folk refer to it as “a big balloon with a small straw”, meaning it can hold large volumes but extraction can be slow at the moment they’re urgently needed. Maybe that feature could be re-engineered away if the £2bn investment happens. Or maybe a different storage solution would be preferable – more on-shore salt caverns, for example, which comprise most of the rest of Britain’s storage capacity. Or the economics favours other ideas in the Neso report: expanding interconnector capacity to the continent, or building more liquefied natural gas import terminals (Britain only has three). Or a combination of the above. The future of North Sea gas production is not incidental to this debate either. The basin may be in long-term decline, but the rate of decline matters (and Jackdaw alone would be 6% of domestic production). If the gas system operator knows the stuff is coming, that is storage by proxy. The point is that a joined-up strategy is needed. Gas still accounted for 35% of the UK’s total energy demand in 2024, according to government statistics. Even if it dwindles to next to nothing for the purposes of electricity generation by 2030 with the rollout of renewables, demand for gas for domestic heating will be slower to shift, as there are 24m households with a gas connection. The short-term question for Fahnbulleh, then, is whether to pay Centrica to fill Rough for this winter. The argument in favour is that storage on the continent, which Britain usually taps during the cold months, is projected to be low after the Hormuz disruption. There is a risk of price spikes. A fuller Rough might dampen them. The longer-term issue is the overall strategy to decide where the gas will come from in 2030. The storage question is central, and so is the policy on North Sea production. There is a strong sense that both have been dodged until now. The government’s interim response to its own “gas system in transition” review is due within weeks. Real choices have to follow because none of these projects – more connectors, more terminals, or more storage – will be quick to build if they are needed. In the meantime, that “emerging risk” of a supply crunch has, presumably, already emerged a bit further.

title: The Hegseth budget: defense secretary’s $1.5tn ask reveals military-industrial chaos
summary: The Pentagon head proposed a 44% increase – to second world war levels – while ‘woke’ programs are gutted
body: When the ‘“department of government efficiency” was raging unchecked through the federal government in February 2025, the US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, ordered his subordinates to find cuts of 8% a year for the next five years, theoretically trimming a total of $340m from the Pentagon budget. The disclosure briefly sent Palantir and defense stocks tanking – though soon the plan evaporated, as if it never existed. Instead, Hegseth is now demanding a $454bn increase for 2027 military spending, to a total of almost $1.5tn, in what he describes as a “generational investment”. Adjusted for inflation, that would for the first time match – and even exceed – US military spending at the height of the second world war during national mobilization, according to a chart prepared by the Center for Strategic and International Studies. The US defense budget is in what may be one of its messiest phases in recent memory. That’s because at the same time Donald Trump and Hegseth are making the push for the unprecedented increase in the defense budget – a 44% jump – they are also insisting on extracting emergency funding for the unpopular and disastrous war under way in Iran. The war needs to be funded separately from the main Pentagon budget, which is why Hegseth is running in essence a two-pronged campaign on Capitol Hill: on the one hand, to cajole and bully Republicans to fund, for the relatively short term, the Iran war, and, on the other hand, to pour a historic amount of financing into the already vast military enterprise he presides over with his deputy, the private equity tycoon Stephen Feinberg. $75bn in spare change Hegseth and chair of the joint chiefs of staff, Dan Caine, faced a skeptical Senate appropriations committee on Tuesday in a hearing that was intended to focus on the Iran war part of the military budget push. Hegseth is asking for $67bn in emergency defense funds for his department for 2026. The ranking Democrat on the committee, Patty Murray, pointed out how much of the spending the Trump administration is asking for as an emergency seems uncorrelated to the flailing operations in Iran. “$800m for the national guard to patrol DC even longer,” she listed. “Over $1bn for the army to patrol the southern border, which is [the Department of Homeland Security’s] job. $900m to support the administration’s illegal boat strikes in the Caribbean.” Hegseth answered by blaming his predecessors: “Why this kind of spending is needed is because of the gross negligence and neglect of the Biden administration.” It’s widely known inside and outside the Pentagon that because of the way the Iran war has been run, the US is running desperately short of what it needs in case of a new military crisis. But with extraordinary obstinacy, Hegseth appears to insist any shortages at all can be blamed solely on Joe Biden and past military leaders. The missiles fired and depleted during Iran, in this construct, are not his fault. There’s yet another kink that is making some senators wary: last year the Hegseth Department of Defense already received a $156bn bump under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. And half of that, about $75bn, is still unobligated – with no detailed plans for how it’s to be spent. By Pentagon standards, it’s spare change, but that’s nearly the value of the entire US Department of Education budget. Questioned about it in the hearing, Hegseth didn’t have any details about that $75bn, though he claimed there was actually a blueprint for where the money will go. “I’d have to get you the exact numbers,” he testified. The Iran war through 30 September Hegseth disclosed a new estimate about the cost of the Iran war – $37.5bn. But he was vague. He said that that would include expenditures up to the present “for certain aspects”, but hedged that the estimate included military pay, operations and maintenance, and other costs “through September 30”. Becca Wasser, a defense expert at the Center for New American Security, said Hegseth’s comments appear to be “a little bit of a signal that the war is still probably going to be going on by September 30”. Messy efforts to fund wars are not new: back during combat operations in Iraq and Afghanistan, the two Bush administrations, the two Obama administrations and the first Trump administration tagged the combat costs on to the defense budget under what were called “overseas contingency operations” (OCO) funds. Biden, Wasser said, shifted Ukraine spending to emergency supplemental requests rather than OCO funding, and Trump in his second term seems to be following that route on Iran. One huge break from the past: Trump defense department spending is being steered to so-called reconciliation – a legislative mechanism outside normal appropriations which strips the senators of the chance to filibuster a measure. Wasser of the Center for New American Security said: “That’s what makes it incredibly unique.” Late on Wednesday, the House passed a $1.15tn bill for a section of the White House request – which may move it forward, but experts say it’s still nowhere close to something that will clear the Senate. The real money, that $1.5tn, dwarfs the costs of the Iran war. While much of it is for the usual tanks and planes and hardware and training, there’s a good chunk for the pet projects of the tech bros and private equity financiers now running the department. The military’s head of research and engineering is the former Uber executive Emil Michael, who oversees artificial intelligence. As the Guardian reported earlier this year, Michael, while in his first months in office, made millions in profits, between 400% and 4,800%, just on his investment in Elon Musk’s xAI company. The Trump budget plans to steer $58.5bn to AI. About $20bn is targeted for the office of strategic capital, a defense department agency that functions as a private equity arm of the Pentagon. The powerful deputy secretary of defense, Stephen Feinberg, former head of Cerberus Capital, has a former employee of Cerberus presiding over the office. It’s the same agency that committed $620m last year to a mining company backed by a fund tied to Trump’s son Donald Jr. The Trump administration did nod in only one apparent way to fiscal constraints. Without apparent irony, the administration said it had identified $1.6bn in defense reductions related to DEI. “The budget,” the White House said earlier this year, “continues to eliminate millions of dollars in wasteful and egregious spending related to diversity, equity and inclusion and other ‘woke’ programs.”
